title: Temporal conditions of work environments
paperName: Paper 3 - Specialist Options: Approaches, Issues and Debates
summary: 9990 Organisational — shift work, flexible hours, and circadian effects on performance.
learningObjectives: Explain circadian rhythms and peak performance times.
Analyse effects of shift work and jet lag on workers.
Evaluate flexible and remote working arrangements.
Assess work-life balance and temporal autonomy.

simpleExplanation.title: Timing is everything
simpleExplanation.summary: When people work affects alertness, errors, and health — night shifts disrupt body clocks; flexible hours can improve satisfaction.
steps: Shift work — fatigue, accidents, health risks.

flashcards.back: Roughly 24-hour biological cycle regulating sleep, alertness, and body temperature — peak alertness often late morning for diurnal humans.
Sleep disruption, fatigue, higher accident rates, cardiovascular and digestive problems from desynchronised body clock.
Both harm health; rapid rotation prevents adaptation; forward rotation (morning→evening→night) often preferred.
Higher autonomy, better work-life balance, reduced lateness — can improve job satisfaction (Herzberg motivator: responsibility).
Eliminates commute time, allows scheduling around peak productivity — but blurs work-home boundaries.
Individual difference in morningness-eveningness — 'larks' vs 'owls' perform best at different times.

sections.content: **Temporal conditions** (4.4.2) — cite **shift work health effects** and **flexibility benefits**. Connect **circadian science** to accident data (4.4.3).

sections.question: A chemical plant uses rotating night shifts. Error rates spike between 02:00 and 04:00, yet management refuses flexitime for day staff citing 'fairness.' Analyse using circadian rhythms and link to safety.
sections.solution:
**Circadian rhythm:** Core body temperature and alertness **dip at night** — performance on **monitoring tasks** falls in the **02:00–04:00 window** (circadian trough), increasing **human error** risk.

**Shift work:** **Rotating shifts** prevent full **adaptation** — workers remain **desynchronised** from natural sleep-wake cycle, causing **fatigue** and **microsleeps**.

**Safety (Reason's Swiss cheese):** Fatigue is one **hole** aligning with **procedure lapses** and **equipment complexity** → accidents (see 4.4.3).

**Flexitime for day staff:** Separate issue from nights but improves **day-worker satisfaction** (JCM **autonomy**) without fixing night errors — need **scheduled breaks**, **light exposure**, **limit consecutive nights**.

**Evaluation:** **Chronotype** means some 'owls' cope better on nights but most do not. **Economic cost** of shift premiums vs **accident cost** must be weighed. Flexitime **fairness** argument is weak — different roles have different temporal demands.
